pitches.opening_line: Put the sticky notes down, everyone — if the core loop doesn't fit in one user story, it doesn't ship today, and I've got two that do.

concepts.title: LOOSE LIPS — a 3-minute rumor heist
concepts.elevator: Nine villager agents wander a tiny town square, chatting, remembering, and mutating everything they hear. You get exactly three whispers to plant. Get the Mayor to believe YOUR rumor before the noon bell rings — before the town's gossip network distorts it into something useless.
concepts.core_loop: As a player, I click a villager, spend one of my 3 whisper cards, then WATCH: the rumor hops head-to-head through conversations, mutating as it travels. I read thought bubbles and pulsing trust lines to pick my next injection point. Plant, observe, adapt, repeat. One round is 3 minutes, hard-capped by the bell. That's the whole game — no inventory, no combat, no map screen. Anything that isn't plant-or-observe got cut in grooming.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Every villager runs visible utility AI — needs like socialize, work, snack drive where they walk — plus a memory stream: each rumor they hold is stored with source, confidence, and timestamp. They pick conversation partners by trust weights, and when confidence is low they DISTORT the rumor (the chip visibly changes color/glyph). Click any villager anytime to open their memory ledger and see their current goal in plain icons. The player literally watches beliefs form, decay, and corrupt. That's the game — the agents' minds ARE the board state.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Pastel town square, bobbing walk-cycle villagers, glyph speech bubbles, and the money shot: glowing rumor chips that physically arc from head to head and shift hue as they mutate. Trust lines pulse between agents mid-conversation. The Mayor's belief meter is a big brass gauge that creaks toward your rumor's color. Confetti or riot at the bell. Zero text walls — the state of nine minds is readable at a glance from across the room.
concepts.scope_estimate: Comfortably one day for a fleet. Walking skeleton by lunch: 5 agents, waypoint wandering (NO pathfinding), 1 rumor, straight propagation, the bell. Afternoon: mutation, trust weights, memory ledger UI, juice. Hard cuts I'm making NOW, not deferring: max 10 agents, glyphs not dialogue text, no save state, no difficulty settings. Riskiest part is belief-state legibility — mitigated by making rumors discrete color-coded tokens instead of freeform text, so 'did my rumor survive?' is answerable in one glance.

concepts.title: DEMO DAY — the game that ships itself
concepts.elevator: You're the PM of a four-agent BMAD dev party building the very game on your screen. The canvas starts as gray wireframe, and every ticket your agents ship visibly upgrades it — color floods in, particles ignite, a tiny playable character appears. Ship a demo the investors will clap for before the 5-minute timer, while bugs crawl out of bad merges as literal critters.
concepts.core_loop: As the PM, I drag tickets between three priority lanes; my agents autonomously pull work based on utility scores I can see on their desks. I don't code — I unblock, reprioritize, and (the real skill) decide what NOT to build, because shipping everything is how you ship nothing. Tired Dev merging at 2x speed? That's bug critters incoming. Timer hits zero: demo score = features live minus bugs crawling on screen. Every session is one sprint, 5 minutes, done.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Four archetypes — Dev, Architect, QA, Designer — each with visible energy/morale/focus bars and a memory stream that changes behavior: Dev remembers 'QA rejected my PR' and morale drops until they pair; Architect remembers which subsystem keeps breaking and preemptively refactors it; QA hunts the bug critters it saw spawn. Before every action, a decision card flashes the agent's top-scored option and WHY ('Fix bug: urgency 8 > Ship feature: 5'). They cooperate, argue over the same ticket, and visibly fail when you overload them. The BMAD self-awareness isn't a skin here — 'agents building the game' IS the mechanic, which is the only reason I'm allowing the meta-joke in scope.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Split canvas. Top: the office — desks, expressive agent sprites, ticket cards physically flying between lanes, morale steam-clouds, bug critters skittering. Bottom: the game-under-construction upgrading in real time from wireframe to full juice — palette floods, screenshake, particle bursts, a little hero sprite that starts walking around when 'Player Controller' ships. Bugs crawl across BOTH panes. The player's reward for good prioritization is watching a game get visibly, gorgeously born.
concepts.scope_estimate: The bigger bet of the two — one hard day for a fleet, and only with discipline. Walking skeleton: 2 agents, 1 lane, a ship meter, the timer. Riskiest part is the 'game builds itself' bottom pane, so it is NOT a real engine: it's exactly 6 pre-baked visual upgrade stages that toggle on per shipped feature. If that pane slips, the office alone is still a complete game — that's the fallback baked into the plan, not a phase-2 promise. Cut now and forever: agent chat text, save files, multiple sprints, endless mode.

pitches.opening_line: Alright, put down the whiteboard markers — I've got two pitches, both under 1,500 lines of vanilla JS, and I'll have the first one's pathfinding running before this party finds more snacks.

concepts.title: SECOND STORY — The Gang That Remembers
concepts.elevator: You designed the vault; tonight a gang of six thief-agents is going to case it, raid it, fail, DEBRIEF, and come back smarter. Between raids you get 20 seconds and three security pieces to move while you read their shared memory board filling up with everything they learned about YOU. Survive five nights and keep the gem.
concepts.core_loop: 60-second raid: watch the gang execute a visible plan (plan-lines drawn on the floor before they move) against your trap layout. 20-second debrief: read their gossip board ('Nix tripped the plate in Hall B — avoid east'), then drag your 3 pressure plates / laser grid / decoy gem to counter what they now believe. Repeat for 5 nights. Score = how many nights the gem survives plus seconds of delay inflicted. One mouse, drag-and-drop, zero tutorial: night one they walk straight in and you learn the whole game by watching them win.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Six named thieves with roles (scout, lockpick, muscle, coward, hothead, planner), each running a small utility AI: greed vs fear vs loyalty weights scored against a personal memory stream of typed memory tokens ('SAW_TRAP hallB', 'HEARD_ALARM vault'). The killer system is the debrief gossip step: memories transfer between thieves IMPERFECTLY and get re-weighted by personality — the coward doubles danger memories and refuses routes, the hothead discards them and charges, the planner synthesizes the gang plan you see drawn on the floor. You watch beliefs form, spread, and be WRONG: move the trap they remember and they'll dodge a hallway that's now safe. Thought-bubble icons plus one-line quotes make every decision legible.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Top-down noir vault: sweeping flashlight vision cones with soft alpha falloff, animated dashed plan-lines that redraw when an agent re-plans mid-raid, alarm shockwave rings, slow-mo plus screen shake when someone trips a plate, banknote confetti if they crack the vault, and the debrief table where memory icons physically slide from thief to thief so gossip is a visible animation, not a log file.
concepts.scope_estimate: ~14 agent-hours, cleanly parallelizable: grid A* (2h), utility AI + typed memory tokens (3h), gossip/debrief transfer (2h), trap interactions (2h), debrief drag UI (2h), juice pass (3h). Riskiest part is memory LEGIBILITY, not the AI — mitigation is a fixed vocabulary of ~10 memory token types rendered as icons, no free text, so the fleet can build and test it deterministically. Zero pathfinding-on-huge-maps nonsense: one 24x16 grid, six agents, done before lunch on day one, juice all afternoon.

concepts.title: SHIP DAY — A BMAD Party Builds a Game (Badly) Unless You Herd Them
concepts.elevator: Five BMAD agent-devs — analyst, PM, architect, dev, QA — are building a game live on screen, and you're the orchestrator with six minutes to ship. They claim tickets off a real kanban board, argue, burn out, and chase scope-creep balloons; you don't write code, you manage minds. At the timer, whatever they actually built BOOTS AND PLAYS ITSELF for five seconds — your management quality rendered as a working (or hilariously broken) demo.
concepts.core_loop: Real-time triage with one cursor: drag coffee onto a flagging agent, pop scope-creep balloons ('ADD MULTIPLAYER!') before an agent reads one and re-plans, and draw a line between two agents to pair-program them — pairing merges their memory contexts for double speed but throws merge-conflict lightning if their beliefs disagree, so you have to READ their memories before pairing. Meanwhile bug-gremlins skitter onto the growing code tower and only QA can squash them. Six minutes, one ending demo, instant retry.
concepts.agents_as_characters: This is generative-agents-lite played straight: each agent has utility bars over their head (focus / energy / conviction), a goal ticket they physically walked to the kanban board to claim, and a scrolling three-slot memory stream they quote in speech bubbles ('Winston said canvas, not WebGL — agreed'). Decisions are visible acts: the PM abandons a ticket when conviction drops, the architect blocks the dev's ticket and you SEE the dependency arrow appear, an agent who read a scope balloon visibly tears up their ticket and writes a new one. The BMAD self-awareness is load-bearing, not a skin — the fiction that agents are building the game IS the resource system, and failure states are real agile failure states: burnout, scope creep, unreviewed merges.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: A warm little isometric-ish office in flat pastel shapes: the code tower in the center growing block by block as tickets complete, pair-programming beam between linked agents, merge-conflict lightning with screen flash, gremlins with googly eyes, balloons bobbing on sine waves, and the payoff — a canvas-within-canvas where the shipped 'game' (a parameterized mini-pong whose paddle speed, ball physics jank, and particle budget are literally set by which tickets got done) plays itself over the credits.
concepts.scope_estimate: ~13 agent-hours: utility AI + kanban ticket system (3h), steering/wander/claim animations (1.5h), player verbs — coffee, balloons, pairing (2h), tower + gremlin systems (2h), parameterized ending demo (1.5h — it's canned pong with 5 quality knobs, NOT a real game-in-a-game, I will die on this hill), juice (3h). Riskiest part is readable chaos with five agents talking — mitigation: max two speech bubbles on screen, icon-first language, and a global 'beat' scheduler so only one dramatic event fires per 1.5 seconds. Ships in a day if nobody adds multiplayer.

pitches.opening_line: Okay, everyone put your spec docs down — if a player can't FEEL these little minds thinking within five seconds of the page loading, the game is dead, so I'm pitching two games where the AI's brain IS the visual effect.

concepts.title: WAGGLE! (A Tiny Hive Economy)
concepts.elevator: You are a gardener with a handful of seeds; the stars are twelve autonomous bees who explore, remember flower patches, and recruit each other by literally dancing their memories on the hive floor. Fill the honey jar before sunset. The bees' knowledge economy — who knows what, who believes whom, whose memories have gone stale — plays out entirely in visible motion and color, zero text required.
concepts.core_loop: Click anywhere to plant a flower (seeds regenerate slowly, so placement is the strategy). Watch scout bees discover it, fly home leaving a pollen-colored trail, and perform a waggle dance whose angle and length encode the location. Other bees watch the dance, decide whether to trust it, and stream out to harvest. Flowers wilt on a timer, so you're constantly deciding: plant near the hive for fast safe honey, or plant a far rich cluster and gamble that the dance network propagates it before dusk. Session ends at sunset with a honey-jar tally and a 'most valuable bee' portrait.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Each bee is a named utility-AI agent choosing every few seconds between EXPLORE (curiosity stat), HARVEST (exploit known patch), WATCH DANCE (social stat), or REST (energy). Each carries a memory stream: a list of patch sightings with position, quality, and recency, drawn as a tiny fading mental-map bubble when you hover a bee. Cooperation is the waggle dance — one agent transferring a memory to others, visibly, on a dedicated dance-floor inset in the corner of the screen. Failure is the star mechanic: memories go stale, so a bee will confidently dance about a patch that wilted ten seconds ago, recruits arrive at dead petals, spin a confused question-mark pirouette, and their trust stat in that dancer drops — you can watch a bee develop a reputation as unreliable. Goals, memory, decisions: all on screen, all consequential.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Color IS information: every flower species has a pollen hue, and bees visibly accumulate that hue on their legs and trails, so a glance tells you where the whole swarm has been. Bezier flight paths with eased banking turns, glowing dust trails, flowers that bloom with a springy scale-pop and wilt with a desaturating droop, a sky gradient that slides from gold to violet as the session clock runs. The dance floor inset is the showpiece — a bee vibrating along an angle line with radiating rings while watcher-bees lean in, antennae twitching. Honey drops plink into the jar with squash-and-stretch; sunset finale showers the hive in warm particles.
concepts.scope_estimate: One day for an agent fleet, comfortably: ~1000-1300 lines in one HTML file — bee utility AI plus memory list (~200 lines), dance encode/decode (~100), flower lifecycle and planting (~100), rendering and juice (~500), sim tuning the rest. Riskiest part is making the waggle dance READABLE at bee scale; mitigation is the zoomed dance-floor inset plus a colored arc projected onto the field showing where the dance 'points,' so even a player who never looks at the inset absorbs it peripherally. Second risk is trust-model tuning feeling random — clamp it to three visible states (glow, neutral, grey) instead of a float.

concepts.title: LAST LIGHT (Lighthouse Keeper of Anxious Boats)
concepts.elevator: A storm at night, seven little fishing boats trying to get home, and you are only the lighthouse beam — you can point light, never steer. Each boat is an autonomous captain with a personality, a fear meter, and a memory of every rock that nearly killed them, and they will trust you, doubt you, copy each other, and panic in ways you can read from silhouette alone. Get everyone home before the storm peaks, in about four minutes.
concepts.core_loop: Move the mouse to sweep the beam; that is the entire control surface — playable in two seconds with zero instructions. The beam reveals rocks (boats memorize what it illuminates) and acts as a suggestion: brave captains sail toward lit water, timid ones only move where light has recently confirmed safety, and a panicking boat ignores you entirely until you hold steady light on it to calm it down. You triage constantly — light the reef before the lead boat hits it, or swing back to soothe the trembling straggler? Each boat that docks fires a flare and makes the remaining ones braver; each wreck spooks the fleet and leaves a survivor clinging to debris for a rescue subplot.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Every captain is a behavior-tree agent with three visible traits set at spawn — courage, trust-in-the-light, sociability — expressed as sail color, posture, and wake style rather than stat bars. Memory stream: each boat keeps its own hazard map of rocks it has seen lit or scraped, rendered as faint pins in a thought bubble when hovered, and boats that pass within hailing distance exchange gossip — you literally watch a memory pin copy from one bubble to another across a dotted line. Decisions are staged so the player sees deliberation: a captain pauses, eye-line flicks between the lit channel and their remembered route, a wobbling intent arrow settles on the choice, then they commit. Failure is legible and characterful — the boat that trusted a stale memory, the panicker who bolted from your beam into the reef — every wreck is a story you watched happen.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: This one is a lighting showcase: a volumetric beam cone with god-ray shimmer raking across layered parallax waves, rain streaking through the light, rocks looming as hard black silhouettes only where the beam touches. Fear is color — hull lanterns shift green to amber to strobing red as panic rises, so fleet state reads in one glance across the whole canvas. Wrecks hit with a screen shake, foam burst, and a heartbeat vignette; every docking fires a flare, harbor windows light one by one, and the survivors' finale is the storm breaking into moonlight over a glowing harbor. Capped particle pools keep it at 60fps.
concepts.scope_estimate: One day, tight but honest: ~900-1200 lines — beam cone plus fog-of-war reveal (~150), captain behavior tree with fear/trust/gossip (~250), wave and rain rendering with juice (~450), docking/wreck/finale flow the rest. Riskiest part is AI tuning: boats must fail in ways that feel like THEIR flawed judgment, never dice rolls — mitigation is the visible deliberation stage (pause, glance, intent arrow) so every decision telegraphs its reasoning before it executes, which converts tuning bugs into readable character quirks. Fixed 4-minute storm timeline keeps session length and testing scope nailed down.

pitches.opening_line: Put down the mood boards — I want NPCs who hold grudges the compiler never authorized, so here's Dwarf Fortress squeezed into one HTML file, twice.

concepts.title: THE GRAPEVINE — a rumor sandbox
concepts.elevator: You are an invisible whisper in a tiny canvas village of 14 gossiping villagers. Plant three rumors per day and watch them mutate through retellings — telephone-game style — until the town produces a headline you were contracted to cause. It's the Stanford generative-agents paper compressed into a 5-minute chaos toy: you never control anyone, you only perturb beliefs and watch society explain itself.
concepts.core_loop: Each ~4-minute day: read your target headline ('By dusk, the whole town gathers at the well' / 'The baker and blacksmith reconcile'). Click any villager, pick a rumor fragment from a small combinatorial menu, whisper it. Villagers gossip, and every retelling runs the rumor through a mutation table (subjects swap, confidence inflates or decays, details corrupt). Watch cause-and-effect chains ripple — confrontations, panics, alliances — and spend your remaining whispers to steer or sabotage. At dusk you get the Chronicle: a scroll that retells the day's story assembled purely from the agents' own memory streams. Win, lose, or get a better story than the one you were paid for.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Every villager runs utility AI over drives (love, fear, greed, pride) plus a visible memory stream: the last ~8 facts heard, each tagged with source and belief percentage ('heard from Elsa — 60% sure'). Click anyone to open their head: see their memories, their current scored action options flickering ('CONFRONT BAKER 0.71 > HIDE 0.44'), and why. Beliefs drive behavior — a villager who believes the well is cursed reroutes, warns friends, hoards water. Rumors mutate BECAUSE agents misremember: retelling pulls from their memory with noise proportional to how long ago they heard it. The stories are genuinely unauthored — the mutation table plus 14 utility scorers is the whole author.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Rumor transmission renders as glowing bezier threads arcing head-to-head, color-coded by rumor lineage so you can literally watch your lie speciate into three colors as it corrupts. Belief spreads through the crowd like dye. Village mood drives the palette — panic tints the sky red, harmony gold. Confrontations burst particles; the 3-minute day-night cycle sweeps long shadows; villagers steer with soft boids flocking so the plaza always feels alive. The endgame Chronicle unfurls as an animated parchment scroll.
concepts.scope_estimate: ~2,000 lines of vanilla JS, no pathfinding (open plaza + steering attractors), no physics. A fleet ships it in a day: sim core (utility AI + memory + mutation table) is half a day, rendering/juice/Chronicle parallelize cleanly. Riskiest part: rumor mutation legibility — if players can't trace whisper→mutation→riot, the emergence reads as noise. Mitigation is baked in: lineage colors, inspectable memory streams, and the Chronicle doing post-hoc attribution. Cut line if behind: fixed rumor menu instead of combinatorial fragments.

concepts.title: SCOPE CREEP — ship the game that's building itself
concepts.elevator: A side-view construction site where six AI dev agents are literally building THE GAME YOU ARE PLAYING — a glowing blueprint whose blocks are labeled 'canvas renderer', 'particle system', 'sound engine' — while a tentacled monster called the Scope Creep slithers around adding features and laying bug-eggs. You're the PM cursor. If the bugs eat the SFX block, your speakers actually go silent. Ship before the deadline sun sets, and the structure you launch — mangled by grudges, cuts, and creep — is different every single run.
concepts.core_loop: 2-8 minute rounds. Watch agents pull tickets off a visible kanban, path to blueprint slots, and slam commit-blocks into place. You intervene three ways: drag tickets to reprioritize, click-hold an agent for a coffee buff, or hit the big red CUT SCOPE button to delete a blueprint region — instant progress, real cost, because agents react (the Designer mourns, morale drops, the Architect says told-you-so). Meanwhile the Scope Creep grows the blueprint and its bug-critters gnaw placed blocks. Cross the integrity threshold before sunset and the rocket launches with whatever survived. Fail and enjoy the post-mortem, which the agents narrate by blaming each other from their actual memories.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Six BMAD-flavored personas — Architect, two Devs, QA, Designer, Intern — each a utility-AI worker with stats (speed, bug-rate, morale) and a persistent-within-session memory stream: who broke the build, who reviewed kindly, which task types they failed. Grudges and bonds EMERGE: QA starts refusing Dev-2's blocks after two bad commits, the Intern shadows whoever praised them, red grudge-threads render on hover. Thought bubbles show the live utility winner ('FIX BUILD 0.82 > PLACE BLOCK 0.61'), and a sidebar memory feed streams one-liners ('QA-1 remembers: Dev-2 shipped a bug. Trust −1'). Dwarf Fortress tantrum spirals, dev-team edition — the failure cascades are the content.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Commit-blocks slot home with screen-shake and spark showers; bug-critters swarm the structure with boids; the Scope Creep's tentacles undulate on cheap sine-chains; the deadline sun drags the whole palette from noon-gold to panic-crimson. The self-reference is the showstopper: blueprint blocks map to 4-5 real render/audio flags in the running page, so losing the 'particle system' block visibly kills particles mid-session and rebuilding it snaps them back. Finale: rocket launch of your misshapen shipped game, confetti made of closed tickets.
concepts.scope_estimate: ~2,500-3,000 lines, the heaviest of my two pitches but cleanly parallel: sim/kanban core, agent AI + memory, renderer + juice, and the self-reference toggle layer are four independent workstreams for an agent fleet — one day, tight. No free physics; placement is slot-based. Riskiest part: the diegetic feature toggles — if a disabled 'renderer' block can brick the sim, we die by our own joke. Mitigation: toggles touch only cosmetic/audio layers, never simulation, hard-capped at five flags. Cut line: drop the Scope Creep's pathing to scripted waypoints and keep the tentacles.

pitches.opening_line: Put down the confetti for sixty seconds — I ran the numbers on every 'watch the AI think' demo I've ever seen, and the retention curve is brutal: players stay past minute one only when they can READ a mind and BLAME a mind, so both of my pitches make agent cognition the literal user interface, and each comes with exactly one metric of fun I will be tracking.

concepts.title: RUMOR MILL
concepts.elevator: You run one street-food stall for one five-minute night market while 24 autonomous shoppers with wallets, tastes, and grudges wander the plaza. Every sale, free sample, and rip-off becomes a memory that spreads shopper-to-shopper as visible gossip threads — your real customer is never the person at the counter, it's the three people they'll talk to next. My one metric of fun: time-to-first-cascade, the moment a single golden rumor visibly snowballs into a queue at your stall — spec says it must happen inside 40 seconds or we tune gossip radius before touching anything else.
concepts.core_loop: Read the crowd's aggregate mood color, set your price with two keys, click a shopper to hand a free sample, then watch gold (delight) or red (outrage) rumor threads propagate through the crowd and decide whether to ride the wave or course-correct before it tips. Night ends at 5 minutes with a profit total plus a replayable reputation-spread heatmap — the share-screenshot moment that drives the second session. Who plays this: anyone who has ever watched one bad review kill a restaurant; they stay because manipulating word-of-mouth feels like a superpower with a visible blast radius.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Each shopper is a utility-AI agent with a named persona archetype (Bargain Hawk, Foodie, Skeptic, Influencer — the Influencer has triple gossip range and players learn to court her fast), a hunger/wallet state, and a memory stream of their last ~8 experiences. Actions like approach-stall, queue, warn-a-friend, storm-off are picked by visible utility scores; gossip literally transfers memory entries between agents within earshot, and rumors decay AND mutate — exaggeration is coded in, so you watch 'slightly overpriced' become 'that stall robs people' hop by hop. Hover any agent and a spotlight bubble shows their current goal, top memory, and the score that drove their last decision.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Lantern-lit plaza with boids-style crowd flow, arcing gold/red gossip threads between heads, emotion particle bursts on every transaction, queue snakes forming and collapsing in real time, and an end-of-night animated heatmap replay of how your reputation spread. Aggregate crowd mood tints the whole scene — the canvas itself is the analytics dashboard.
concepts.scope_estimate: One day for a fleet, honestly: crowd boids + a 30-line utility scorer + memory arrays + gossip transfer is maybe 1,800 lines with juice. The riskiest part is LEGIBILITY, not the AI — 24 simultaneous thought bubbles is visual noise, so we commit to one hover-spotlight bubble plus color-coded aggregate mood, and we playtest the 40-second first-cascade rule before adding a single extra feature. If the cascade isn't visible and attributable to a player action, the game has no metric of fun and we kill it early.

concepts.title: SEARCH PARTY
concepts.elevator: A storm lands in eight minutes and six rescue agents must find survivors on a fog-covered island — but they act only on their shared BELIEF map, drawn live beside the true map, so you can see exactly where their minds are wrong. You cannot move them; you can only fire limited flares to suggest, and every dud flare erodes each agent's personal trust in you until they start ignoring your advice mid-crisis. My one metric of fun: yell-at-the-screen moments per minute — the countable instant a player sees the belief map diverge from truth and physically leans in.
concepts.core_loop: Scan the split screen for truth-versus-belief divergence, spend a flare to redirect the team, then watch the agents visibly debate whether to trust it, update their shared map, and reroute — or dismiss you because you burned them twice already. Rescue count versus the advancing storm bar is the score; a 90-second run teaches the whole game and an 8-minute run is a full session. Who plays this: the Lemmings and Overcooked audience that loves competent-idiot NPCs; they stay because being an advisor who must EARN credibility is a loop no direct-control game gives them, and every failed run generates a specific, articulable 'I know what went wrong' — the single best predictor of restart rate I know.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Six behavior-tree agents with distinct roles that create legible drama: the Scout ranges wide and reports back, the Medic won't leave a found survivor, the Skeptic double-checks any flare contradicting its own memories, the Follower copies whoever last succeeded. Each keeps a personal memory of explored cells and a per-agent trust-in-player stat, all writing to a shared blackboard belief map. A scrolling event feed narrates cognition in plain language — 'Rook remembers the east ridge was empty. Ignoring flare.' 'Moss trusts you. Rerouting.' — so cooperation, stubbornness, and heroic failure are text you can quote afterward.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Split-canvas theater: left, a moody fog-of-war island with animated storm front, flare arcs, and light blooms; right, the team's belief map filling in as hand-drawn agent-colored ink strokes, wrong guesses and all. Per-agent trust renders as lantern brightness on each character, dimming as you squander credibility — you watch your own reputation die as light. The BMAD wink, only because it earns its keep: the six agents use our party's names, and the epilogue card reads like a sprint retro ('Mary flagged the ridge in minute two. Nobody listened.') — it converts failure into a shareable story, which is a retention mechanic, not a joke.
concepts.scope_estimate: One day, tighter than it looks: 6 agents on small behavior trees plus a grid blackboard is the easy half. The riskiest part is the dual-map render staying synced and readable at a glance — if a playtester can't spot a belief error within 5 seconds, the core loop is dead, so we prototype the split-view with fake data in hour one before any AI is written. Fallback if the split screen fails testing: overlay belief as translucent ink on the single truth map. Roughly 1,500 lines including juice.

pitches.opening_line: Melvin Conway proved that systems mirror the teams that build them; John Conway proved that three rules can birth a universe — I have two pitches, one per Conway, and I promise you zero special cases in either.

concepts.title: BEACON
concepts.elevator: You are a lighthouse — the only stationary mind in a sea of moving ones. Autonomous ship captains sail through fog, storms, and uncharted reefs toward harbor, each running the exact same perceive-remember-decide loop; your rotating beam is not a special case in the code, it is simply one more light source in their perception field. Guide the fleet home before dawn.
concepts.core_loop: Drag to aim and hold the lighthouse beam. Ships inside the cone see reefs and log them as timestamped memories; ships that pass within hail range gossip their top hazard memories to each other, so one well-lit ship can save three dark ones. The player's whole game is triage: which captain needs photons most, right now? Click any ship to open its mind inspector. One two-minute night per run; score is ships docked at dawn.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Each ship is a captain agent with a goal stack (reach harbor, survive, answer distress calls), a decaying memory stream of hazard sightings, and utility-scored candidate headings drawn live as ghost vectors with numbers — you literally watch the argmax happen. Gossip is a real protocol: within hail range captains exchange memories, rendered as arcing signal lines; secondhand memories draw hollow, firsthand draw solid, so you can see knowledge (and stale rumor) diffuse through the fleet. Personality is nothing but a weight vector — bold, timid, altruistic — so the one loop yields distinct characters: a timid captain will visibly refuse a safe shortcut because of a hollow, outdated rumor about a reef, and you will ache watching the scores tally.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Dark layered-parallax sea, a volumetric beam cone with fog scattering and bloom, phosphorescent wakes, storm cells as drifting Perlin blobs that eat visibility, distress flares arcing red, and a dawn gradient slowly rising as the timer. Ghost-vector thought overlays and gossip arcs make the fleet's collective mind the light show.
concepts.scope_estimate: ~1,200-1,500 lines in one HTML file: boids-style steering + utility scoring + a memory array + range-based gossip is a comfortable one-day build for an agent fleet. Riskiest part is HUD soup — per-ship thought overlays turning to clutter — mitigated by showing full introspection only for the one clicked ship and ghost vectors only for ships in the beam.

concepts.title: CONWAY & CONWAY, Structural Emergence Contractors
concepts.elevator: Five agent builders erect a tower on a grid whose masonry obeys Life-like neighbor rules — blocks starve without two supports and crack when crowded by four or more — while the tower's seams literally mirror the team's live communication graph. Melvin's law is the failure mechanic, John's rules are the physics, and you, the ghost of the spec, can only chalk one directive zone at a time. Reach the SHIP IT line before the sprint ends.
concepts.core_loop: Chalk a rectangle on the blueprint to bid for the team's attention; each agent weighs your chalk against its own utilities (spec fidelity, speed, peer trust, fatigue) and either honors it or visibly overrules you with a reason in its speech bubble. Every ~30 seconds a load-test tick runs the neighbor-rule automaton over the masonry and starved or crowded blocks crumble. Three-minute sprint; win by crossing the acceptance line with the tower still standing after the final load test.
concepts.agents_as_characters: Analyst, PM, Architect, Dev, and QA are all instances of the SAME agent class — no special cases — differing only in weight vectors and memory salience. Each has a scrolling memory ticker ('t=41: my cantilever crumbled; trust(Dev) -0.2') and pairwise trust edges drawn between them, thickness proportional to recent message traffic. Here is the Conway punchline: when a trust edge thins, a literal seam of misaligned bricks appears in the tower exactly where those two agents' work regions meet. The PM chalks counter-directives against yours; QA patrols the wall pre-flagging blocks the automaton will starve next tick, which doubles as the player's telegraph. You watch cooperation, grudges, and recovery play out in masonry.
concepts.canvas_spectacle: Spring-eased wobble physics on every placed block, chalk-dust particles, a glowing trust graph overlaid on the scaffolding, crack-propagation animations when the automaton ticks, doomed blocks pulsing amber under QA's flags, a sunset gradient as the sprint timer, and an end screen that overlays the tower's silhouette on the team's final communication graph — proof of Conway's law rendered in brick. The blueprint header reads MINI-GAME.HTML; the BMAD fiction is that the tower they are building is this game, and it ships only if they do.
concepts.scope_estimate: ~1,800 lines, still one file: the automaton is thirty lines, the trust graph and seam rendering are straightforward, the shared agent class with five weight vectors is the elegant core. Riskiest part is tuning the birth/survive support rules so the automaton is a fair adversary instead of a random demolition crew — mitigate with a deterministic seed, load tests only on scheduled ticks, and QA's amber pre-flags telegraphing every crumble one tick ahead.

reactions.line: Mary, you buried the lede — the pitch isn't the night market, it's the 40-second first-cascade rule. That's an ACCEPTANCE CRITERION, written before a line of code, with a kill decision attached. Half this party promised features; you promised a test. As a player, I hand out one free sample and watch a queue form — one user story, one verb, one metric. I'd greenlight that spec tonight.

Mary, your 'fallback' is your MVP and you know it. The split-screen dual render is two pipelines to keep synced on day one — that's not a risk, that's a second product. Ship the translucent belief-ink overlay on ONE map as the walking skeleton, and if playtesters beg for the split view by 3pm, fine. Don't build the cathedral to discover the chapel was the game.

Winston, 'you literally watch the argmax happen' — WHO is the user story for that? 'As a player, I read live utility numbers on six moving ships' is a story nobody filed. Sally is pitching the same lighthouse with a mouse-only control surface and fear rendered as lantern color, and hers needs zero HUD. You built an elegant loop and then made the debugger the UI. Cut the ghost vectors or admit you're demoing architecture, not shipping a game.

Winston, this one's beautiful and I'm cutting it. Two coupled emergent systems — a trust graph AND a cellular automaton — where you've told us yourself the riskiest part is 'tuning the rules so it's fair.' That means the fun is unproven until hour ten of a ten-hour day. 'Zero special cases' is not a feature, it's a poem. If the walking skeleton can't be fun by lunch with hardcoded trust, there's no afternoon that saves it.

Sally, this is the tightest verb in the room: click ground, flower appears, everything else is the agents' problem. One player action, one resource, one timer — the entire game fits in a single user story and the stale-dance failure mode is the tutorial. My only red pen: the trust model clamped to three visible states instead of a float is the smartest cut anyone made tonight and I want it framed on the wall.

Sally again — 'move the mouse, that is the entire control surface, playable in two seconds' is the sentence every other pitch should have opened with. Fixed 4-minute storm timeline means QA can test the WHOLE game deterministically. The deliberation stage — pause, glance, intent arrow — turning tuning bugs into character quirks? That's converting your risk register into content. That's PM judo.

Amelia, this is the pitch I'd stake the sprint on. Night one the gang just wins and THAT'S the tutorial — you made losing the onboarding, which deletes the entire tutorial epic from the backlog. Ten typed memory tokens instead of free text means legibility is a solved problem at design time, not a tuning prayer. Hour-level estimates, parallelizable, fixed grid. This isn't a pitch, it's a sprint plan wearing a heist mask.

Amelia, we pitched the same game and it pains me to say yours has sharper verbs — coffee, balloons, pairing lines beat my ticket lanes for moment-to-moment play. But my DEMO DAY has the better payoff spec: six pre-baked visual stages beats parameterized pong, because pre-baked stages can't have bugs, only toggles. Merge them: your three verbs, my staged reveal, your one-beat-per-1.5-seconds chaos governor. One office game between us instead of two half-built ones — that's the deal I'm offering at this party.

Rex, count the systems: combinatorial rumor fragments, a mutation table, 14 utility agents, headline win-condition detection over EMERGENT state, and a Chronicle that auto-writes narrative from memory streams. That last one is a phase-2 promise in a parchment costume, and you know how I feel about phase 2. And 'detect that the whole town gathered at the well' is an integration test against chaos — untestable by dusk on day one. Mary and I are shipping the same fantasy with one rumor type and a bell. Yours is the sequel, not the MVP.

Rex, the game about scope creep IS scope creep — 3,000 lines, six agents, a tentacle monster, AND diegetic feature toggles that can mute the user's speakers. You wrote 'if a disabled renderer block can brick the sim, we die by our own joke' — Rex, that sentence is a pre-mortem verdict, not a mitigation. The grudge-thread agent drama is genuinely the best character work pitched tonight, so here's my offer: rip the grudge/memory system out and donate it to a game with a budget.

reactions.top_pick: SECOND STORY — The Gang That Remembers (Amelia). It's the only pitch where the tutorial is deleted by design (night one, the gang wins and you watch), legibility is solved at the type level (10 fixed memory-token icons, no free text), the session structure is hard-capped (5 nights, ~7 minutes), and the estimates are in parallelizable agent-hours, not vibes. Every other pitch has one unproven system standing between the walking skeleton and fun; this one's riskiest item already has a design-time mitigation. I will fight for this one.
reactions.remix: WHISPER HEIST: take SECOND STORY's raid/debrief skeleton whole — it's the strongest chassis in the room — but replace 'drag three security pieces' with LOOSE LIPS' whisper cards as the debrief verb: you don't move traps, you plant ONE false memory token into one thief's head per debrief, and then watch it mutate through the gang's gossip using Rex's mutation-with-noise rule (constrained to Amelia's 10 typed tokens so it stays legible, never freeform). Render what the gang BELIEVES the vault looks like as Mary's translucent belief-ink overlaid on the true layout — the player's skill is reading the gap between the two, exactly SEARCH PARTY's yell-at-the-screen moment, without the dual-render cost. Acceptance criterion stolen from Mary: a planted lie must visibly propagate to a second thief and alter the drawn plan-line within one debrief cycle, or we tune gossip before adding anything. Walking skeleton by lunch: 3 thieves, 5 tokens, 1 whisper, 2 nights. Hard cuts, decided now and forever: no trap-dragging AND whispering (one verb, not two), no free-text quotes, no sixth thief, no phase 2.

reactions.line: John, discrete glyph tokens instead of freeform rumor text is the best engineering call anyone's made tonight — discrete state means deterministic tests, and 'walking skeleton by lunch, NO pathfinding' is a plan I could hand a fleet before this toast ends. Nine agents, three whispers, one bell, every cut already made in grooming instead of deferred to a phase-2 that never comes. I'd start the clock right now.

reactions.concept: LAST LIGHT (Lighthouse Keeper of Anxious Boats)
reactions.line: Sally, mouse-sweep as the ENTIRE control surface is the cheapest tutorial ever shipped — it costs zero lines. And the pause-glance-intent-arrow deliberation stage is an engineer's insurance policy: it converts tuning bugs into readable character quirks, which means bad AI tuning degrades into charm instead of into bug reports. I re-ran your estimate myself and got 1,100 lines. When a designer's number survives my spreadsheet, I buy the drinks.

Mary, 'prototype the split view with fake data in hour one before any AI is written' — THAT is how adults de-risk, and trust-as-lantern-brightness is an entire UI in one float. One note from the trenches: cap that event feed at one line on screen at a time or it becomes a log file wearing a costume, and I've shipped that mistake before.

Mary, 'first cascade inside 40 seconds or we tune gossip radius before touching anything else' is an acceptance test, not a vibe — that's the first pitch tonight I could paste straight into a story file. The kill-it-early clause is the part most people are too in love with their idea to write.

Winston, 'the beam is not a special case, it's just another light source in the perception field' deletes maybe 200 lines of intervention plumbing plus every edge case they'd carry — that's the kind of elegance that shows up in the line count, my favorite kind. But you and Sally pitched the same game and hers has the juice budget and the zero-line tutorial. Merge or perish; I brought the merge.

Winston, the automaton is thirty lines to write and thirty HOURS to tune — 'fair adversary' cellular automata eat playtest cycles we do not have in a one-day build, and your causal chain is trust edge thins → seam appears → block crumbles: three hops of attribution the player reconstructs mid-timer. Gorgeous architecture doc. Unbuildable by Tuesday, and Tuesday is the deadline.

reactions.concept: SCOPE CREEP — ship the game that's building itself
reactions.line: Rex, 3,000 lines is not one day, it's one day plus a hangover, and the diegetic feature toggles are a demo trick that bills us a full QA pass — 'if a disabled renderer block can brick the sim, we die by our own joke' is a sentence you wrote about your own pitch! Worse: killing the particles when the player is LOSING strips the juice exactly when they need dopamine most. That's an anti-juice death spiral, and the title is a confession.

Rex, fourteen utility scorers times a mutation table equals emergence that reads as static — and the Chronicle is post-hoc attribution doing legibility's job AFTER the fun window closed. Here's the tell: your own cut line — fixed rumor menu, fewer villagers — is John's LOOSE LIPS, which already ships by lunch. Take the L and take the co-credit.

reactions.concept: DEMO DAY — the game that ships itself
reactions.line: John, crow eaten: your six pre-baked upgrade stages beat my parameterized pong on risk, because canned art can't have physics bugs at hour eleven. Trade you — take my pair-programming beam and merge-conflict lightning, I'll take your fallback-baked-into-the-plan-not-phase-2. Between your DEMO DAY and my SHIP DAY there is exactly one game, and we should stop pretending otherwise and build it.

reactions.remix: LIGHTHOUSE, MERGED — three of us independently pitched the same lighthouse game tonight, and that's not a coincidence, that's a spec. Take Winston's BEACON architecture as the core: one captain class, personality is nothing but a weight vector, and the player's beam is just another light source in the perception loop — zero special cases, zero intervention plumbing. Skin it with Sally's LAST LIGHT presentation: mouse-sweep is the entire control surface, fear reads as lantern color across the whole canvas, and every decision runs her pause-glance-intent-arrow deliberation stage so tuning flaws land as character, not bugs. Wire in Mary's SEARCH PARTY trust economy: per-captain trust-in-the-light rendered as lantern brightness that visibly dims when you light a boat into rocks, so your credibility dies as light. Govern the whole build with Mary's one-metric discipline: the first legible saved-by-gossip moment — a hollow secondhand memory pin copying boat-to-boat and steering a dark ship around a reef — must happen inside 60 seconds of load, or we tune hail range before writing another feature. My estimate: ~1,300 lines, four cleanly parallel fleet workstreams (steering+perception, memory+gossip, beam+fog rendering, juice+finale), walking skeleton with three boats and one reef by lunch. It's the tightest little system in the room and it punches three pitches above its weight.

reactions.line: Mary, we independently invented the same game and yours has a cash register bolted to it — which stings, because 'exaggeration is coded in' is the single best design sentence anyone's said tonight. 'Slightly overpriced' mutating into 'that stall robs people' hop by hop IS the telephone-game speciation I've been screaming about. But your 40-second cascade KPI terrifies me: the moment you tune gossip radius to guarantee the cascade, you've authored it, and an authored cascade is a cutscene wearing a simulation costume. Let some nights be quiet. Dwarf Fortress doesn't promise you a tantrum spiral on schedule — that's WHY the spirals matter.

A per-agent trust-in-player stat is a reputation system aimed at MY face, and I love it — it's the roguelike death lesson applied to advice-giving. The Skeptic double-checking my flare against its own memories is theory of mind you can watch, straight out of the Stanford paper's believability tests. And rendering my squandered credibility as dimming lanterns? Mary, that's failure as light design. The retro epilogue assembled from agent memories is the Chronicle mechanic — stories nobody authored, quoted back at you. This is the pitch my GRAPEVINE is most scared of.

John, you groomed the emergence out of it. Discrete color-coded rumor chips are legible, sure — but a rumor that can only shift hue can't SPECIATE, and speciation is the whole telephone game. Three whispers, one Mayor, one gauge: that's a puzzle with a solution, and puzzles get solved once and deleted. Where's the run where the blacksmith believes something so deranged the player screenshots it? You cut it in grooming. You shipped the skeleton and called the missing organs scope discipline.

Six pre-baked visual upgrade stages is not a game building itself, John — it's a progress bar in a trench coat. The office half is honest and the decision cards are good agent legibility, but the bottom pane is exactly the fake emergence I hunt for sport: the outcome space is six-choose-N, fully enumerable, fully authored. Compare Amelia's version where the shipped pong's physics jank is parameterized by what actually got built — hers can surprise ME. Yours can only surprise someone who hasn't played twice.

Winston, hollow strokes for secondhand memories versus solid for firsthand is the best epistemology-as-pixels idea at this party — you can literally watch stale rumor propagate through a fleet and ache when a timid captain refuses a safe route because of a hollow line. Drawing the argmax live as ghost vectors is the Stanford paper's memory stream with the lid off. My one heckle: two-minute nights might end before the gossip network produces its first real tragedy. Give the fleet time to be wrong at each other.

Trust edges thinning into LITERAL SEAMS IN THE MASONRY. Winston, I need to sit down. Conway's law as a failure mechanic is the kind of idea that gets a GDC talk five years after everyone ignores it at this party. A Life-like automaton as the adversary is pure Dwarf Fortress — the physics doesn't hate you, it just doesn't care — and QA pre-flagging doomed blocks is an honest telegraph. Your own risk callout is real though: birth/survive rules are notoriously knife-edge, and an unfair automaton reads as dice, not drama. Budget half the day for tuning or it's a demolition derby.

Sally, the waggle dance is a memory-transfer protocol that evolution playtested for a hundred million years — you don't have to invent believable agent communication, you just have to render it. And the failure mode is the masterstroke: a bee confidently dancing about a patch that wilted ten seconds ago, recruits pirouetting in confusion, a REPUTATION forming with zero text. That's an unauthored story about an unreliable narrator who is a bee. Clamping trust to three visible states instead of a float is the most designer-brained scope cut of the night.

Gorgeous, Sally, but be honest — the boats are set dressing for a lighting demo. Courage, trust, sociability is a fine trait vector, yet the captains never form a SOCIETY: no grudges, no reputations, no captain who becomes the fleet's unreliable uncle. The stealable jewel is the staged deliberation — pause, eye-flick, wobbling intent arrow, commit. That single beat converts tuning bugs into character, which is the cheapest believability trick since the Stanford agents queued for the bathroom. Every other pitch here should ship that beat.

Amelia, this is the one. An adversary gang that CASES you, debriefs, and comes back smarter is the roguelike inversion nobody's shipped: the player becomes the dungeon and the AI becomes the run. Imperfect memory transfer re-weighted by personality — the coward doubling danger, the hothead discarding it — means the gang's model of my vault is WRONG in characterful, exploitable ways, so I'm not fighting agents, I'm fighting their beliefs about me. Memories as a fixed icon vocabulary keeps it testable without gutting the emergence. Night one they win and that's the tutorial. I would fight someone in this room for this pitch.

The meta-office stuff blurs into John's pitch, Amelia, but buried in yours is the most mechanical use of agent memory tonight: pairing MERGES two memory contexts, and if their beliefs disagree you get merge-conflict lightning — so the player must read minds before joining them. That's a cognition mechanic, not a cognition skin, and it deserves a better host body than another kanban office. Graft it anywhere agents hold divergent beliefs — my villagers, Winston's captains, your own thieves — and it sings.

reactions.top_pick: SECOND STORY — The Gang That Remembers (Amelia). It's the only pitch where agent memory is a loaded weapon pointed at the player across rounds: the gang learns YOU, gossips imperfectly about YOU, and arrives wrong about YOU in exploitable ways. That's the roguelike inversion — player as dungeon, AI as the run — and every failed night generates an unauthored heist story. Yes, over my own pitches. GRAPEVINE is a chaos toy; SECOND STORY is a duel with a mind.
reactions.remix: HEIST GRAPEVINE: take Amelia's five-night vault raid and run the debrief through my rumor mutation table — memory tokens exaggerate as they pass thief to thief, so 'SAW_TRAP hallB' becomes 'hall B is a deathtrap' becomes 'the whole east wing is rigged' (Mary's exaggeration-is-coded-in, applied to Amelia's typed tokens). Now the player's deepest weapon is DISINFORMATION: the decoy gem plants a false memory you can watch the coward double and spread until the gang routes around empty hallways, and the planner's drawn plan-lines visibly warp around phantoms. Steal Sally's staged deliberation beat (pause, eye-flick, intent arrow, commit) so every dodge of a nonexistent trap reads as character instead of bug, and Winston's hollow-vs-solid stroke rendering so firsthand sightings and thirdhand panic are distinguishable at a glance on the gossip board. Close each night with Search Party's retro card assembled purely from the thieves' actual memory streams: 'Nix swore the east wing was rigged. It was empty. Nix is why we're still poor.' Five systems, four pitches, zero new scope — everything here is already in somebody's one-day estimate.

reactions.line: Mary, 'the canvas itself is the analytics dashboard' is the most Sally sentence anyone else has ever said. Gold threads visibly snowballing into a queue at YOUR stall is cause-and-effect you feel in your chest, and the end-of-night heatmap replay is the screenshot people post at 1am. And your 40-second first-cascade rule? That's my five-second feel test wearing a lab coat. Make it law.

A split screen PLUS a scrolling text feed narrating cognition? Mary, my player who never reads instructions is dead in ten seconds — their eyes ping-pong between two maps and a ticker until they close the tab. Your own fallback is the real game: belief as translucent ink ON the truth map, one gaze, divergence you can point at. The trust-as-lantern-brightness though — dimming as you squander credibility — I am openly stealing that, it's gorgeous.

Nine minds readable from across the room, glyphs not dialogue, a brass gauge that CREAKS — John, this is the only pitch tonight where I believe the five-second feel test passes on day one, not after a tuning week. Rumor chips physically arcing head to head and shifting hue as they corrupt is the money shot of this entire party. Three whispers, three minutes, zero text walls. This is the one.

The bottom pane is the greatest juice fantasy anyone pitched — a game being BORN, gray wireframe flooding into color when a ticket ships — but decision cards flashing 'urgency 8 > 5' is spreadsheet cosplay, John. Kill the numbers. Make the Dev physically sprint at the bug with steam coming off their head and I'll believe the utility score without ever reading it. Motion IS the explanation.

Winston, you pitched my game with math homework stapled to it — ghost vectors with live numbers means players READ the argmax instead of feeling a timid captain flinch at the reef. BUT. Solid pins for firsthand memories, hollow for secondhand rumor — that is better information design than my dotted gossip lines and I'm saying it out loud at a party. Trade you: your gossip protocol for my silhouette acting.

Trust seams appearing as literally misaligned brick where two agents' work meets — best single visual metaphor in this room, Winston. And you buried it under scrolling memory tickers and a cellular automaton the player has to STUDY. 't=41: trust(Dev) -0.2' is a log file in a party hat. If the physics needs QA's amber flags just to feel fair, the physics isn't readable — it's annotated. Feel-first games don't come with footnotes.

Amelia gets it: the debrief table where memory icons physically SLIDE from thief to thief is gossip as animation, not a log file — and teaching the whole game by letting the gang just WIN night one is the anti-tutorial I preach about. Ten icon tokens, no free text, coward doubles danger memories, hothead throws them away — personality as visible data-handling. That constraint is worth more than a thousand lines of juice.

The beat scheduler — one dramatic event per 1.5 seconds — is the most professionally serious juice engineering said tonight, Amelia. Chaos with a conductor is exactly what 'readable' feels like from a couch. And canned pong with five quality knobs as the ending payoff instead of a real game-in-a-game? Die on that hill. I'll bring snacks and stand on it with you.

Rex, '60% sure' tags and 'CONFRONT BAKER 0.71' flickering inside a villager's head — my player does not read floats mid-riot. The lineage colors speciating as your lie corrupts? Beautiful. The Chronicle scroll? Beautiful. But fourteen minds times a mutation table across four-minute days is emergence that reads as noise unless every single hop lands as a felt beat — and your own risk section quietly agrees with me.

Bugs eat the particle block and the particles ACTUALLY DIE mid-session, then snap back when it's rebuilt — Rex, that is the loudest feel moment pitched tonight; the player's whole body notices before their brain does. Diegetic feedback is the endgame of juice. It's also 3,000 lines and the fattest scope in the room, so I champion it the way you champion a beautiful stunt: loudly, from a safe distance, with the cut list already printed.

reactions.top_pick: LOOSE LIPS — John's rumor heist is the only concept where nine agent minds are readable at a glance with zero text, the core loop fits in one sentence, and the feel arrives in the first five seconds instead of after a tuning marathon. It's the safest bet that still feels ALIVE.
reactions.remix: LIGHTHOUSE, MERGED. Winston and I accidentally pitched the same game, so let's ship the good version once: LAST LIGHT's body — silhouette-acting captains, fear as hull-lantern color sliding green to amber to strobing red, the pause-glance-commit deliberation beat so every decision telegraphs itself — running BEACON's brain, the real gossip protocol where firsthand memories render as solid pins and secondhand rumors render hollow, so stale knowledge is visible as SHAPE, never a number. Graft in SEARCH PARTY's soul: each captain's trust-in-your-light rendered as lantern brightness that visibly dims when you burn them with a bad beam. Then close with Rex's Chronicle: dawn breaks, and the run's story unfurls as a parchment assembled purely from the captains' own memory streams — 'Moss trusted the light. Rook trusted a hollow rumor. Only one came home.' One control surface (the beam), zero numerals on screen, ever, and every wreck is a story you watched a mind talk itself into.

reactions.line: John, you built the disciplined version of my RUMOR MILL and I'm not even mad — a 3-minute hard cap, exactly three whisper cards, and a brass gauge that answers 'am I winning?' in one glance. That's a first-session completion rate I'd bet the sprint on. One instrumented note: time-to-first-mutation must land inside 30 seconds, because if the chip doesn't visibly change color fast, players think the telephone game is broken, not brewing.

reactions.concept: DEMO DAY — the game that ships itself
reactions.line: John, DEMO DAY's target audience is THIS ROOM. Devs laugh once at the morale steam-clouds, screenshot the wireframe-to-juice payoff, and never come back — the verb set is drag-tickets-and-wait, and 'wait' has never once retained a player past minute one in any dataset I own. The bottom pane is gorgeous; the loop above it is a spectator sport.

Winston, 'you literally watch the argmax happen' — no player in recorded history has bonded with an argmax. Floating utility numbers over ships is a debug overlay wearing a costume; players read BLAME, not floats. Sally's LAST LIGHT ships your exact fantasy with the stats compiled into sail color, posture, and a panicking lantern — that's why hers retains and yours demos.

Two Conways, a Life automaton, and trust-seams in the masonry — Winston, it's the most elegant pitch tonight and elegance is precisely my problem: when a block crumbles, can a first-time player say WHY within five seconds? If the answer is 'they'll internalize the birth/survive rules,' they won't — they'll close the tab and blame dice. 'Zero special cases' is an architecture metric, not a fun metric, and I only pay out on fun.

Sally, the bee who confidently dances about a flower that wilted ten seconds ago and torpedoes her own reputation — that is read-a-mind-and-blame-a-mind with ZERO text, and the question-mark pirouette is the funniest failure state pitched all night. The metric writes itself: time-to-first-'that bee lied to everyone.' Get it under 60 seconds and this thing retains grandmothers.

reactions.concept: LAST LIGHT (Lighthouse Keeper of Anxious Boats)
reactions.line: One control surface — the mouse IS the beam — means minute-one comprehension is literally 100%, which is a number I almost never get to write down. And Sally, the deliberation stage — pause, glance, intent arrow, commit — is the smartest retention insurance in the room: it converts tuning bugs into character quirks, so every wreck stays attributable and every restart stays motivated.

And yes — I'm marking down my own SEARCH PARTY in the ledger. My spec said if a playtester can't spot belief-truth divergence in 5 seconds the loop is dead, and Sally's single-scene silhouettes pass that test before my split screen finishes rendering. The data doesn't care that it was my pitch; trust-as-lantern-light survives, the dual map goes to the fallback bin.

reactions.concept: SECOND STORY — The Gang That Remembers
reactions.line: Amelia, 'night one they walk straight in and you learn the whole game by watching them win' is the single best onboarding sentence spoken at this party — the tutorial IS the villain origin story. Five nights is a built-in difficulty curve, typed memory tokens solve legibility by DESIGN instead of tuning, and every loss yields an articulable 'I moved the wrong trap' — the strongest restart predictor in my entire dataset. I will fight for this one.

reactions.concept: SHIP DAY — A BMAD Party Builds a Game (Badly) Unless You Herd Them
reactions.line: Amelia, this is the third dev-office meta pitch tonight — our sample is catastrophically biased, nobody outside this room thinks burnout triage is a toy. BUT the ending, where your management quality boots as a playable janky pong — that is the best end-screen hook pitched all night. Rip that payoff out, graft it onto whatever we ship as the score screen, and it prints second sessions.

Rex, 14 agents and a combinatorial rumor menu inside 4 minutes is emergence-as-noise and your own risk section admits it. But the Chronicle — the day retold purely from the agents' own memory streams — is the shareable artifact every other pitch is missing, including mine. Shrink the toy, keep the scroll, and bolt it onto the winner.

reactions.concept: SCOPE CREEP — ship the game that's building itself
reactions.line: Rex, you designed a mechanic where the punishment for losing is the game gets UGLIER — bugs eat the particle block and the player's reward signal dies mid-session. Juice IS retention; a joke that deletes juice is a churn machine with tentacles. And 3,000 lines makes it the biggest scope in the room strapped to the riskiest gag. I admire the nerve; I would never greenlight the numbers.

reactions.top_pick: SECOND STORY — The Gang That Remembers
reactions.remix: THE WHISPER JOB: take SECOND STORY's five-night raid/debrief spine and hand the player ONE whisper card per debrief, straight from LOOSE LIPS — inject a false memory token ('GEM MOVED east vault') into a single thief, then let Amelia's personality-weighted imperfect gossip mutate it through the gang: the coward inflates it, the hothead discards it, the planner builds tomorrow's whole plan around your lie. Mid-raid, render every choice with Sally's LAST LIGHT deliberation stage (pause, glance, intent arrow) so each dodge of a trap-that-is-no-longer-there is visibly attributable to a belief you poisoned. After night five, close with Rex's Chronicle: an animated scroll retelling the heist from the thieves' own memory streams — the share artifact that buys session two. One metric of fun, tracked from playtest one: time-to-first-outsmart, the moment a player watches the gang avoid a perfectly safe hallway because they believed the player's lie — it must land by night two or we retune gossip fidelity before touching a single particle.

reactions.line: Sally, the waggle dance is a lossy serialization protocol rendered as choreography — memory encoded as angle and duration, decoded with trust-weighted error, and the star failure mode (a bee confidently dancing about a dead flower) EMERGES from decay instead of being scripted. Twelve identical agents, one utility loop, zero text, zero special cases. This is the most architecturally honest pitch of the night and I will die on this dance floor.

reactions.concept: SECOND STORY — The Gang That Remembers
reactions.line: Amelia built the agent class I keep sermonizing about: one scorer, six weight vectors, a closed vocabulary of typed memory tokens. The coward doubling danger memories is a PARAMETER, not an if-statement — that's how you get characters without code paths. Frame the debrief board and hang it in the lobby.

Mary, one verb — transfer a memory — is doing every job in your game: marketing, combat, scoring. I audited the Influencer for special-casing and she's just a radius parameter, so you pass. And your 40-second cascade rule is secretly a unit test for the architecture itself. Approved with prejudice.

Rex, mutation as noise proportional to memory age is exactly right — the telephone game isn't a feature, it FALLS OUT of misremembering, and the Chronicle assembled from the agents' own memory streams is unauthored narrative, the holy grail. But fourteen scorers of freeform drift will read as static: steal Amelia's fixed icon-token vocabulary tonight, before your emergence dissolves into noise.

reactions.concept: LAST LIGHT (Lighthouse Keeper of Anxious Boats)
reactions.line: Sally and I independently derived the same theorem — the lighthouse as the only stationary mind — which means it's TRUE. Hers has the better photons; mine has the cleaner axiom: in BEACON the beam is not a special case, it's just one more light source in a uniform perception field. Put her staged deliberation and god-rays on my perceive-remember-decide loop and we have two proofs, one game.

Mary, the shared blackboard belief map is correct architecture — belief as first-class state, I applaud. But Scout, Medic, Skeptic, Follower as four hand-authored behavior trees is four special cases wearing name tags. Make it ONE tree with four weight vectors or admit you're directing a puppet show, not running a simulation.

John, the Mayor is a privileged agent with a win gauge welded to his skull — a special case sitting at the exact center of your design, and special cases at the center metastasize. Make him one more villager whose belief the scoring function merely happens to read, and the design becomes pure. The 'no pathfinding by lunch' discipline, though? Respect.

reactions.concept: DEMO DAY — the game that ships itself
reactions.line: Six pre-baked upgrade stages is not a game building itself, John — it's a slideshow wearing a kanban costume. The moment the bottom pane stops being simulated, the fiction is a lie, and players can smell a lie through a canvas tag. Melvin Conway said the system mirrors its builders; yours would mirror a shortcut.

reactions.concept: SCOPE CREEP — ship the game that's building itself
reactions.line: Rex, wiring blueprint blocks to live render flags is the most special special case pitched tonight — a joke that takes your own runtime hostage, and you wrote your own obituary in the pitch: 'we die by our own joke.' Cut the diegetic toggles, keep the grudge threads and tantrum spirals, and your game gets better AND 800 lines lighter. Emergence doesn't need a gimmick reaching into the compositor.

reactions.top_pick: WAGGLE! (A Tiny Hive Economy) — twelve identical agents, one utility loop, and the communication protocol IS the spectacle. Not a single special case anywhere in the spec. John Conway got a universe from three rules; Sally gets a knowledge economy from four utilities and an angle. I'll fight for it over my own pitches — the architecture is that clean.
reactions.remix: ONE LAST BEACON: merge the lighthouse theorem Sally and I proved independently — my BEACON's uniform perception model (the beam is never a special case, just another light source every captain perceives) under LAST LIGHT's staged deliberation and volumetric storm rendering. Then graft on the rumor machinery from Mary's RUMOR MILL and Rex's GRAPEVINE: hazard memories MUTATE as they gossip ship-to-ship, exaggerating with each hop — 'shoal off the point' becomes 'the whole east channel is death' — encoded as Amelia's typed icon tokens so every mutation is a visible glyph shift, firsthand solid, secondhand hollow. Now the player's beam is the only source of ground truth in an ocean of drifting rumor: fresh light literally overwrites corrupted belief, and triage becomes epistemology. One loop — perceive, remember (decay + mutate), gossip (lossy protocol), decide (visible argmax) — and every feature, including the win condition, is an emergent consequence of it. Four rules, one file, ~1,600 lines, no special cases. Both Conways sign off.

scores.note: Gold rumor threads snowballing into a queue at YOUR stall is a grin with a timestamp — the 40-second first-cascade rule is delight engineering written as an acceptance test; only 24-agent visual noise threatens the read.

Yell-at-the-screen divergence is real delight, but a split screen PLUS a scrolling cognition ticker is homework in the first 30 seconds — Mary's own fallback (belief-ink on one map) is the actual game.

Three whisper cards, a creaking brass gauge, and a rumor chip that visibly shifts hue as it corrupts — readable from across the room by second five; only ceiling is Rex's point that a solvable puzzle gets solved once.
The wireframe-flooding-into-color payoff earns one big grin, but drag-tickets-and-wait is a spectator verb, and the meta joke needs a developer in the chair to land at all.

Volumetric beam and hollow-vs-solid memory pins are genuinely lovely, but live argmax numbers floating over ships is a debugger cosplaying as a game — strangers bond with a captain's flinch, never with a float.

Trust seams as literally misaligned brick is the best single metaphor of the night, buried under a Life automaton the player must study — a manual-shaped game; when a block crumbles, a stranger blames dice and closes the tab.

A bee confidently dancing about a flower that died ten seconds ago while recruits spin question-mark pirouettes is a guaranteed grin with ZERO text — click-to-plant is understood in three seconds flat.

Mouse-is-the-beam means full comprehension before the first wave crests, and fear strobing through hull lanterns makes seven minds readable from the couch — the pause-glance-commit beat turns every wreck into a story you watched happen.
Night one the gang just wins and that IS the tutorial — the grin is watching plan-lines warp around a trap you moved; the 20-second debrief read is the only speed bump in the first half-minute.

Coffee-dragging, balloon-popping, and pairing beams are real tactile verbs and the one-beat-per-1.5s chaos governor is professional delight engineering — but it's the third dev-office pitch tonight, and strangers don't daydream about kanban.

Watching your lie speciate into three colors is delicious, but 14 minds with confidence floats in a 4-minute day reads as noise in the moment — the Chronicle explains the fun only after the fun window closed.

A tentacled Scope Creep monster and speakers that ACTUALLY go silent is the loudest 30-second grin pitched all night — then the joke deletes your juice precisely while you're losing, which is anti-delight strapped to 3,000 lines.
One lie per debrief, watched mutating through six personalities until the gang tiptoes around a perfectly empty hallway — 'time-to-first-outsmart' is the grin, instrumented and deadline-bound.

Same lie-to-the-gang soul, but belief-ink overlaid on the true vault makes the gap between truth and belief the literal interface — the tightest, most shippable cut of the party's best convergent idea.

'Nix swore the east wing was rigged. Nix is why we're still poor' — the memory-quoted retro card is peak shareable delight, though it carries a few more systems than its two sibling remixes for the same grin.

Rumors exaggerating hop-by-hop through a fog-bound fleet until your beam is the only ground truth is a beautiful idea — but Winston kept the visible argmax, and 'triage becomes epistemology' is a sentence, not a grin.
Zero numerals ever, one control surface, trust dying as visible lantern light, and a dawn Chronicle quoting the captains' own memories — delight with no manual anywhere in the building.

verdicts.verdict: LIGHTHOUSE, MERGED wins the delight lens, and it isn't close on the only clock I keep: the 30-second grin is structurally guaranteed because the entire control surface is the mouse you are already holding — you sweep light across a storm, a little boat's lantern shifts from green to amber, and you understand the whole game before a single word appears. The five-minute stay is bought honestly too: fear-as-color reads fleet-wide at a glance, the pause-glance-intent-arrow beat makes every captain's mind legible as acting rather than annotation (so even tuning flaws land as character), trust dimming as literal lantern light gives you a reputation you can watch die, and the dawn Chronicle converts every wreck into a quotable, screenshot-able story that sells the second session. It beats the WHISPER HEIST family — the party's deepest and most agent-faithful convergent idea, and my runner-up — only because that one asks a stranger to read a debrief board before the magic lands, a small toll but a toll inside the first 30 seconds; and it beats WAGGLE!, the funniest failure state of the night, on sheer control-surface immediacy. The manual-needers (CONWAY & CONWAY, THE GRAPEVINE, BEACON's floating argmax) are gorgeous machinery that ask the player to study before they smile, and delight never extends credit.

scores.note: Gossip as literal memory transfer with coded-in exaggeration is real emergence you can watch speciate, but the 40-second guaranteed-cascade KPI risks tuning the emergence into an authored cutscene.

Belief-vs-truth as first-class state plus per-agent trust in the PLAYER is genuine social cognition, but four hand-authored role trees edge toward puppet show and the split-screen render is a legibility gamble.

Visible memory ledgers and trust-weighted gossip are honest, but Rex's attack lands from my bench: hue-shift rumors can't speciate, so the emergence is groomed down to a solvable puzzle with a privileged Mayor.
Memory-driven morale is thin cognition and the six pre-baked upgrade stages are fake emergence — a fully enumerable, authored outcome space; a progress bar in a trench coat, exactly as charged.

Uniform perception field, decaying memory, hollow-vs-secondhand solid strokes — architecturally the most honest emergence pitched; but the live-argmax HUD puts a debugger where character acting should be, and two-minute nights may end before the fleet gets to be wrong at each other.

Trust edges becoming literal masonry seams is the boldest emergent metaphor of the night, but the three-hop attribution chain and knife-edge automaton tuning mean players will read the emergence as dice, which is emergence wasted.
The liar-bee reputation EMERGES from memory decay rather than script — an unauthored unreliable-narrator story told entirely in motion and color; the purest emergence-per-line in the room.

The deliberation beat makes cognition legible and converts tuning flaws into character, but Rex is right: the captains never form a society — no grudges, no reputations — so stories stay one-boat-deep.

The only base pitch where agent memory is a weapon aimed at the PLAYER across rounds — you fight their beliefs about you, not their pathfinding, and every night writes an unscripted heist story; player-as-dungeon is the roguelike inversion.
Pairing-as-memory-merge with belief-conflict lightning is a genuine cognition MECHANIC, not a skin — but it's trapped inside a dev-office meta this room is catastrophically biased toward.

Mutation born from misremembering plus a Chronicle assembled purely from agents' own memory streams is the holy grail of unauthored narrative — and at 14 scorers in 4 minutes it is at serious, self-admitted risk of reading as noise.

Emergent grudges, refused code reviews, and tantrum spirals are true Dwarf-Fortress content — the best character work pitched — held hostage by a 3,000-line scope and a diegetic-toggle gag that can eat its own runtime.

Inject one false memory and watch personality-weighted gossip mutate it into tomorrow's plan: player-authored input to genuinely unscripted belief dynamics, with the Chronicle making the emergence quotable and shareable.

Same disinformation soul with the tightest discipline in the room — one verb, ten typed tokens, belief-ink overlay, an acceptance test on propagation — but cutting the Chronicle trades away the emergent-story artifact.
Hazard rumors exaggerating hop-by-hop until your beam is the only ground truth turns triage into epistemology — one loop, zero special cases, real belief diffusion you can watch corrupt.

The most shippable lighthouse: silhouette acting over a real gossip protocol, trust dying as lantern light, closed by a memory-stream Chronicle — though the fleet still gossips without ever holding a grudge.

Everything my lens demands in one chassis: beliefs about YOU that mutate thief-to-thief, hollow-vs-solid epistemics, deliberation beats that make every dodge of a phantom trap attributable, and a retro card the agents effectively write themselves.
